{"language": "markdown", "code": "## 8. Action Economy & Items

Three pools per turn, plus one category outside them all.

**Movement** — your budget, splittable around your Action. Assess costs 1m from this pool.

**Action** (one per turn):
- Attack (light or heavy)
- Hold (commit to parry on the enemy's turn)
- Use an Action Item
- Manage a track (smother, scrape, pressure)

**Fast Action** (one per turn):
- Use a Quick Item

Heal-and-attack is real: Quick Items are Fast Actions, so vial-and-swing works. The lever is potency — Quick Items are deliberately weak; anything strong costs a full Action, competing with attacking. Big heal OR attack; small heal AND attack.

**Reactions** sit outside all pools — things that happen on someone else's turn. Retreat (§12) and Abandon-All (below) live here.

### Limb-gating

| Requires | Staggered | Severed |
|---|---|---|
| Weapon arm (attacks, holds, item use) | −1 die / heavies disabled this turn | Can't wield that side; off-hand only, reduced damage, two-handers unusable |
| Legs (movement) | Budget halved | Crawl only (1m), can't kite, prone (standing costs your Action) |
| Head | Dazed — Discombobulation 1 turn, can't Assess | Dead (head severance always lethal) |
| Torso | n/a — torso stagger is just heavy damage | Dead |

A staggered leg takes kiting off the table — the triangle narrows by one option, yours or theirs.

### Items

**Quick Items** (Fast Action — weak, frequent):
- **Wrap** — clears 2 Blood Loss stacks
- **Salts** — clears 1 Insanity tick
- **Tonic** — small flat heal (~10% max HP)
- **Scour** — clears 1 Corrosion stack without costing your turn

**Action Items** (full Action — strong, situational):
- **Source-water flask** — big heal, but adds an Influence stack. Power for vulnerability.
- **Cautery iron** — fully clears Blood Loss; stationary and exposed while using (no movement this turn).
- **Thrown vial** — applies Corrosion/Burning at range.
- **Charm** — one-shot buff (extra die, widened parry window); Insight-gated to use (§11).

**Reactive / Instant** (usable anytime, even mid-enemy-turn, no slot):
- **Abandon-All** — the panic word, the psychic tether home. Spoken or thought — needs only a working mind and voice, no limbs. Mechanically: Retreat (§12) — forfeit the Purse, keep character/limbs, instant, free, usable between any two actions including mid-resolution of an attack. The only counter — a gag/silence effect or an enemy that severed your voice — is rare, boss-tier, always telegraphed.
- **Distress flare / ally beacon** — same category; multiplayer hook for later.

**Passive / Equip** (no slot, always active) — trinkets and build-around gear. Modifies numbers only; often Insight-tier-gated to equip.

---

## 9. The Visceral System (parry + stopwatch)

content.thinking:
The system doesn't have a "bonus action" mechanic—it uses Movement, Action, Fast Action, and Reactions instead. Fast Action (for Quick Items like Wrap) is a separate pool from the main Action, so he can use both in the same turn, meaning healing and attacking together is definitely possible.

Now I need to define what Dragged actually does mechanically since I created it. The creature is physically gripping and pulling Lloyd toward deeper water, so the effect should restrict his Movement as a core consequence. Dragged isn't a full stun—it's restrictive but leaves Action and Fast Action available, which aligns with what we just established about him being able to attack the tendril. Movement is locked at zero while gripped, but Abandon-All remains an escape option since it only requires a working mind and voice. Each turn the grip persists, the creature hauls him closer to whatever danger lies ahead.

summaries.summary: Clarifying action economy mechanics and healing-attack combinations.
Clarified movement restriction mechanics and action availability simultaneously.

content.text:
**Dragged**, as I'm defining it now that it needs a real definition instead of just flavor:

- **Movement budget: 0** while gripped — he can't reposition under his own power, only wherever it hauls him.
- **Action and Fast Action are unaffected** — he's restrained, not stunned. That's exactly why punching the tendril was legal last turn.
- **Persists until broken** — Heavy attack on the grip (already on the table), a Strength contest, or Abandon-All (which explicitly works even with limbs restrained — voice and mind only, per §8).
- **Escalating cost each turn it's not broken:** it keeps hauling him toward deeper water every turn it holds. I haven't decided what's waiting at the end of that haul yet, and I'm not going to pretend I have — but it's not nothing, so breaking it sooner is better than later.

On the "bonus action" question — this system doesn't have that category. The two real pools are **Action** and **Fast Action**, and they're separate: he could attack (Action) *and* use a Quick Item (Fast Action) same turn if he had one worth using. Right now his only Quick Item is the Wrap, which clears Blood Loss — he's got none, so it wouldn't do anything this turn.

So: Light, Heavy, or something else for the Action — and Fast Action's available too if you want it, though nothing in inventory actually helps right now.
can Lloyd load a cartridge into one of his metal knuckles with a fast action and then try a heavy hit on the tentacle holding him?

content.thinking:
Loading a capsule counts as a Fast Action, so combining it with a Heavy attack in the same turn is within the action economy—the Powder Charge detonation check triggers when the Heavy lands. Now I'm working through resolving that Heavy attack against the tendril.

The Heavy deals 57 base damage (44 + 0.75×ESV with ESV at 17). If the detonation check hits, it deals an additional 85-86 damage plus 1 Discombobulation to the target; if it misses, Lloyd takes 1 self-inflicted Blood Loss stack instead. Either way, the capsule is consumed. The Heavy attack itself breaks the Drag grip outright regardless of whether the detonation fires correctly—that's determined by the physical force of the impact alone.

Now I need to set a stopwatch window for this detonation check. Since this is a self-initiated action during combat without an enemy tell to anchor on, I'm working through how to establish the timing parameters.

content.text:
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"## 9. The Visceral System (parry + stopwatch)

The signature mechanic. You need a stopwatch (phone is fine).

**Assess.** Spend 1m of movement: the DM reveals an enemy's tell and rough timing of its next heavy (and, if relevant, its movement pattern). Clarity scales with Insight — low gives a ballpark ("about a second and a half"), high gives a tighter, truer call. You can then hold your action to parry on the enemy's turn.

**The parry:**
1. Enemy telegraphs a heavy. Attack type maps to a window — target time and tolerance. Examples: Lunge 1.50s ±0.20 · Overhead 2.00s ±0.15 · Sweep 1.20s ±0.25. Faster, tighter = more dangerous.
2. Start the stopwatch — no charge cost, no cap on attempts. *(Session zero ruling: charges removed; parries are gated by timing and a real opening only, to stop players hoarding attempts for bosses instead of playing the system.)*
3. Pause inside the window.

**Outcomes:**
- **Inside → PARRY.** Enemy opened. Visceral: your next strike deals 1.5× damage (plus Insight mods), knocks the enemy down, ends its turn, costs it movement next turn. Some enemies retaliate when viscerald (§16).
- **Outside → WHIFF.** Eat the heavy in full plus a punish (DM's pick: crit back, apply a track, or stagger you a turn). A whiff hurts more than blocking — that's the gamble. Don't reuse a tell: a second, different heavy on the last one's timing gets you killed.

Insight widens windows and unlocks visceral mods (§11): Bloody, Sustaining, Quickening.

### Post-parry weak points — three tiers

DM assigns per enemy on a simple read: does a parry reliably floor this thing?

- **Tier 1 — Open** (default). Parry floors them; the weak point is yours, no second stopwatch. The parry was the skill check. (Mooks, elites.)
- **Tier 2 — Widened** (some bosses). Opened but not floored. Precision window (§10), widened one tier.
- **Tier 3 — Full double** (bosses, chaotic limbs). Parry stopwatch, then a second precision stopwatch at normal tightness. The ceiling, not the norm.

**Table fallback:** if real-time timing bogs down, replace any stopwatch with a d20 reflex roll using Insight as the modifier, same outcomes. Solo AI play uses the stopwatch.

---

## 10. Limbs, Precision & Mutation

{"returncode":0,"stdout":"## 9. The Visceral System (parry + stopwatch)

The signature mechanic. You need a stopwatch (phone is fine).

**Assess.** Spend 1m of movement: the DM reveals an enemy's tell and rough timing of its next heavy (and, if relevant, its movement pattern). Clarity scales with Insight — low gives a ballpark ("about a second and a half"), high gives a tighter, truer call. You can then hold your action to parry on the enemy's turn.

**The parry:**
1. Enemy telegraphs a heavy. Attack type maps to a window — target time and tolerance. Examples: Lunge 1.50s ±0.20 · Overhead 2.00s ±0.15 · Sweep 1.20s ±0.25. Faster, tighter = more dangerous.
2. Start the stopwatch — no charge cost, no cap on attempts. *(Session zero ruling: charges removed; parries are gated by timing and a real opening only, to stop players hoarding attempts for bosses instead of playing the system.)*
3. Pause inside the window.

**Outcomes:**
- **Inside → PARRY.** Enemy opened. Visceral: your next strike deals 1.5× damage (plus Insight mods), knocks the enemy down, ends its turn, costs it movement next turn. Some enemies retaliate when viscerald (§16).
- **Outside → WHIFF.** Eat the heavy in full plus a punish (DM's pick: crit back, apply a track, or stagger you a turn). A whiff hurts more than blocking — that's the gamble. Don't reuse a tell: a second, different heavy on the last one's timing gets you killed.

Insight widens windows and unlocks visceral mods (§11): Bloody, Sustaining, Quickening.

### Post-parry weak points — three tiers

DM assigns per enemy on a simple read: does a parry reliably floor this thing?

- **Tier 1 — Open** (default). Parry floors them; the weak point is yours, no second stopwatch. The parry was the skill check. (Mooks, elites.)
- **Tier 2 — Widened** (some bosses). Opened but not floored. Precision window (§10), widened one tier.
- **Tier 3 — Full double** (bosses, chaotic limbs). Parry stopwatch, then a second precision stopwatch at normal tightness. The ceiling, not the norm.

**Table fallback:** if real-time timing bogs down, replace any stopwatch with a d20 reflex roll using Insight as the modifier, same outcomes. Solo AI play uses the stopwatch.

---

## 10. Limbs, Precision & Mutation

content.text:
194	## 5. Status Tracks
   195	
   196	Most conditions are tracks — counters that build (0–10) rather than switches. Defaults below; tune to taste.
   197	
   198	**BLOOD LOSS** — 1 stack per qualifying slashing/piercing hit or hazard. Each new stack deals immediate bonus damage equal to the new stack total (0→1: +1; 1→2: +2; five consecutive cuts = 15 bonus total). No passive drain between hits. Cap 10 → triggers Rupture, resets to 0. Clear: bandage, cautery, or a pressure action (a Wrap clears 2 stacks, §8).
   199	
   200	**RUPTURE** (triggered only, at Blood Loss 10) — 25% of Max HP direct damage, staggers you a beat, resets Blood Loss to 0. Low Rally recovery — ten unmanaged stacks are meant to stick. **(untested)**
   201	
   202	**INSANITY** — the "character acts without you" meter. On taking damage, witnessing something wrong, or failing a fear save: Save Roll (below). Fail → DM controls you one turn (freeze, flee, strike the nearest body, speak to something that isn't there). High Insight raises this. *Not the same as Insight.*
   203	
   204	**BURNING** — ticking damage each turn. Spreads to oil, cloth, steam-soaked surfaces. Action to smother. Heat sources can re-ignite you.
   205	
   206	**DISCOMBOBULATION** — a Tick, not a track. Applied at 1–3 (DM's call by severity: scrambled sign = 1, the source-spring stair = 3), counts down 1 at end of each of your turns automatically. While active the DM may misdescribe exits, reverse movement, strip reactions, or scramble attack directions — pick what fits. A fresh application refreshes to the new value if higher. Cap 3. (Long-term cousin: Fugue, §13 — a Track, not a Tick.)
   207	
   208	**INFLUENCE** — some enemies suggest rather than strike, verbally or otherwise — a spoken command, or just a pull, an ache, a wordless want. Builds as a Track like Blood Loss, but **uncapped past 10** — no fixed overflow trigger, no Rupture-equivalent. Instead, **the severity of compelled effects available to the DM scales with stack count**: low stacks might mean lingering near a source or a minor compulsion; higher stacks can escalate to dropping a weapon, fleeing, self-harm, or striking an ally. This is deliberately open-ended DM discretion, not a fixed table — calibrate severity to stack count and use judgment so it doesn't wreck pacing. On a failed save, obey the effect (or the enemy's spoken command, if it has one) on your next turn. Susceptibility rises with Insanity and Insight.
   209	
   210	- **First save trigger:** a source's stacks don't force a save until the 2nd stack lands. *(Session zero ruling.)*
   211	- **Clear conditions:** a successful resist alone does not clear the stack while the source remains active — it only avoids that turn's compulsion. Full clear happens once the source is eliminated (line of sight fully broken). **With multiple simultaneous sources, full clear requires ALL active sources gone** — killing one of several just stops that one from adding further stacks. *(Session zero ruling.)*
   212	
   213	**CORROSION** — stacking decay. Damage at start of your turn = stacks × 2. Cap 5 (10/turn). Stacks tick down 1 at end of turn ONLY IF you spent an action managing them (douse, scrape, neutralise); otherwise they hold. Sources: acid, spores, fouled water, coatings. Stacks viciously with Burning.
   214	
   215	### The Save Roll (Insanity & Influence)
   216	
   217	One formula, both saves. Roll 1d20; succeed on:
   218	
   219	**≥ 10 + (current track value) − ⌊Resolve ÷ 10⌋ + ⌊Insight ÷ 2⌋** (+ situational mods from gear or the enemy). **(untested)**
   220	
   221	*Worked example: level 1, Resolve 8, Insight 1, second Influence stack → 10 + 2 − 0 + 0 = 12. Later: Resolve 30, Insight 6, Influence 6 → 10 + 6 − 3 + 3 = 16 — Resolve pulls its weight, Insight spends it right back.*
   222	
   223	---
   224	
   225	## 6. Rally

Most conditions are tracks — counters that build (0–10) rather than switches. Defaults below; tune to taste.

**BLOOD LOSS** — 1 stack per qualifying slashing/piercing hit or hazard. Cap 10 → triggers Rupture, resets to 0. Clear: bandage, cautery, or a pressure action (a Wrap clears 2 stacks, §8).

**RUPTURE** (triggered only, at Blood Loss 10) — 25% of Max HP direct damage, staggers you a beat, resets Blood Loss to 0. Low Rally recovery — ten unmanaged stacks are meant to stick. **(untested)**

**INSANITY** — the "character acts without you" meter. On taking damage, witnessing something wrong, or failing a fear save: Save Roll (below). Fail → DM controls you one turn (freeze, flee, strike the nearest body, speak to something that isn't there). High Insight raises this. *Not the same as Insight.*

**BURNING** — ticking damage each turn. Spreads to oil, cloth, steam-soaked surfaces. Action to smother. Heat sources can re-ignite you.

**DISCOMBOBULATION** — a Tick, not a track. Applied at 1–3 (DM's call by severity: scrambled sign = 1, the source-spring stair = 3), counts down 1 at end of each of your turns automatically. While active the DM may misdescribe exits, reverse movement, strip reactions, or scramble attack directions — pick what fits. A fresh application refreshes to the new value if higher. Cap 3. (Long-term cousin: Fugue, §13 — a Track, not a Tick.)

**INFLUENCE** — some enemies suggest rather than strike, verbally or otherwise — a spoken command, or just a pull, an ache, a wordless want. Builds as a Track like Blood Loss, but **uncapped past 10** — no fixed overflow trigger, no Rupture-equivalent. Instead, **the severity of compelled effects available to the DM scales with stack count**: low stacks might mean lingering near a source or a minor compulsion; higher stacks can escalate to dropping a weapon, fleeing, self-harm, or striking an ally. This is deliberately open-ended DM discretion, not a fixed table — calibrate severity to stack count and use judgment so it doesn't wreck pacing. On a failed save, obey the effect (or the enemy's spoken command, if it has one) on your next turn. Susceptibility rises with Insanity and Insight.

- **First save trigger:** a source's stacks don't force a save until the 2nd stack lands. *(Session zero ruling.)*
- **Clear conditions:** a successful resist alone does not clear the stack while the source remains active — it only avoids that turn's compulsion. Full clear happens once the source is eliminated (line of sight fully broken). **With multiple simultaneous sources, full clear requires ALL active sources gone** — killing one of several just stops that one from adding further stacks. *(Session zero ruling.)*

**CORROSION** — stacking decay. Damage at start of your turn = stacks × 2. Cap 5 (10/turn). Stacks tick down 1 at end of turn ONLY IF you spent an action managing them (douse, scrape, neutralise); otherwise they hold. Sources: acid, spores, fouled water, coatings. Stacks viciously with Burning.

### The Save Roll (Insanity & Influence)

content.thinking:
I'm thinking through whether Discombobulation works symmetrically for enemies the same way it does for players—the status description is written from the player's perspective, but something like Powder Charge explicitly inflicts it on targets, which suggests it should apply to enemies too.

The key distinction is that Discombobulation doesn't skip a turn entirely like a stun would; instead, it's a debuff that scrambles actions at the DM's discretion through misdescription, reversed movement, stripped reactions, or scrambled attack directions. For enemies, some effects like misdescribed exits wouldn't apply, but the movement and action scrambling would work well as a confusion mechanic.

Looking at Powder Charge specifically, it applies "1 Discombobulation (Tick)" with no severity modifier stated, which means it's locked at severity level 1—the lowest tier—rather than leaving it to the DM's judgment like the general Discombobulation rules suggest.

Since this creature is rooted and lacks PC-style reactions, the most fitting severity 1 effect would be a scrambled attack direction—its grab attempt goes wide or fails to connect properly because it's disoriented from the explosion at its contact point, which feels narratively satisfying and mechanically concrete.

summaries.summary: Scrutinizing whether Discombobulation affects enemies identically to players.

For this thing — rooted, no real movement to reverse, no reactions to strip — **scrambled attack direction** is the one that lands: the detonation went off right where it was gripping, and its whole read on where Lloyd actually is gets thrown off by it.

It surges again, tendrils lashing out toward roughly where he *was* mid-drag rather than where he actually ended up post-break. **It whiffs entirely.** No damage, no new grab — just a wasted, confused turn.

Architected movement-cost system, identified design conflict requiring resolution.

content.text:
Solid direction — it turns movement into an actual currency instead of just positioning. Here's the draft, rebalanced where your instincts needed a nudge:

---

**§7/§8 REWORK DRAFT — Movement as Action Fuel**

**Base movement budget: 5m → 8m.** Movement is now the single per-turn currency that both repositioning *and* actions draw from.

**Action movement costs** (paid from the budget, in addition to the Action slot itself):

| Action | Cost | Fiction |
|---|---|---|
| Light attack | 1m | Plant feet, jab, keep moving |
| Heavy attack | 3m | Full stop, wind-up, commit |
| Hold (parry stance) | 2m | Set stance, weight down, wait |
| Manage a track (smother/scrape/pressure) | 1m | A pause, not a production |
| Use an Action Item | 2m | Hands busy, attention split |
| Assess | 1m | (unchanged — already costs this) |

**Fast Actions: 0m, always.** Loading a capsule, quaffing a Quick Item — done on the move, that's the whole point of the category.

**The hard rule:** if you can't pay an action's movement cost, you can't take it. Sprint all 8m and you've got no attack in you this turn. This is the interplay — every meter run is a meter you can't fight with.

**Ripple effects, resolved:**
- **Staggered leg (budget halved):** now bites much harder — 4m budget means a Heavy + 1m of movement, total. Severed leg (1m crawl) = Light attacks only, no Heavy ever. I think that's *good* — leg damage mattering more is on-theme — but flagging it.
- **Cautery iron's "no movement this turn"** now reads as: costs your full remaining budget on use. Cleaner than a special case.
- **Perk hooks** (future, not drafting now): movement refund on kill, first attack each turn costs 0m, +2m after a successful parry, etc. Slot cleanly into this frame.

**PARRY RESTRICTION (separate change, same commit):**
Enemies cannot parry or visceral the player except **bosses and hand-flagged bespoke elites**. Standard archetypes punish whiffs and telegraph heavies, but the visceral loop belongs to the player.
- **Conflict flag:** the **Effigy** archetype (§16) explicitly "parries you, viscerals you" — that's its whole identity as the mirror-match enemy. Options: (a) reclassify Effigy as boss-tier only, (b) keep it as the *one* named exception, or (c) strip its parry and give it something else. Needs your call before commit.

---

Balance check on your numbers: old system was 5m + free attack; new Light turn is effectively 7m-equivalent mobility, new Heavy turn is 5m — so Lights got slightly *more* mobile and Heavies stayed put. That feels right for a skirmisher game. Only thing I'd watch is Hold at 2m — it punishes parry-fishing while repositioning, which might be intended or might feel bad in play. Could drop to 1m.

Proofread away — what's the Effigy call, and any cost tweaks?
effigy can be a boss archetype, sure. what is "Hold"?

Hold is your parry stance — it's the existing mechanic from §9's visceral system, not something new I invented. It's what Lloyd declares when he's read an enemy's tell (usually via Assess first) and wants to attempt the stopwatch parry: he Holds, waits for the wind-up, and you run the timer trying to land inside the window. In = visceral (1.5× damage + knockdown + enemy loses its move). Out = you eat the heavy plus a punish.

It occupies your Action slot for the turn — you're committed to waiting, not swinging. All I did in the draft was assign it a movement cost like everything else: 2m for setting your stance and planting your weight.

The interplay it creates: a Hold turn at 2m leaves 6m of repositioning, so parry-fishing keeps you fairly mobile — versus a Heavy turn at 3m. Dropping Hold to 1m would make the parry game cheaper and more attractive; keeping it at 2m says committing to a read costs you something. Either's defensible — parries are already gated by needing a genuine telegraphed opening, so they're not spammable regardless of the movement price.

Keep Hold at 2m, or drop to 1m?
oh, yep, all good. add it all to the rules, then let me know when we can continue

239	## 7. Movement & Positioning
   240	
   241	Movement is combat's third axis.
   242	
   243	**Budget** set by Endurance (5–11m). Spend before and after your action — in, strike, out — up to the total.
   244	
   245	**Hit-and-run.** Attack and retreat beyond the enemy's reach in one turn. No parry = no visceral opening — chipping, not opening. Safe, slow, low-reward. Good when hurt, out of charges, or whittling something you can't yet open.
   246	
   247	**Kiting.** Enemies have move budgets too. If yours exceeds theirs, stay out of reach and chip. A slow enemy in open space is manageable; a slow enemy that has cornered you is not.
   248	
   249	**Burst-movers.** Some alternate a surge turn (huge ground, often attacking only at the lunge's end) with a recovery turn (barely moving). Read the rhythm: in during recovery, out before the surge. Assess (§9) can reveal movement patterns, not just tells.
   250	
   251	**The triangle:**
   252	- **Trade** — stand and swing. Fast damage, eat hits.
   253	- **Parry** — high risk, high reward. Viscerals and weak points; a whiff hurts.
   254	- **Kite** — move and chip. Safe, slow, no openings, useless against anything as fast as you.
   255	
   256	Good play flows between all three as health, charges, and tempo shift.
   257	
   258	---
   259	
   260	## 8. Action Economy & Items
   261	
   262	Three pools per turn, plus one category outside them all.

{"language": "markdown", "code": "## 7. Movement & Positioning

Movement is combat's third axis.

**Budget** set by Endurance (5–11m). Spend before and after your action — in, strike, out — up to the total.

**Hit-and-run.** Attack and retreat beyond the enemy's reach in one turn. No parry = no visceral opening — chipping, not opening. Safe, slow, low-reward. Good when hurt, out of charges, or whittling something you can't yet open.

**Kiting.** Enemies have move budgets too. If yours exceeds theirs, stay out of reach and chip. A slow enemy in open space is manageable; a slow enemy that has cornered you is not.

**Burst-movers.** Some alternate a surge turn (huge ground, often attacking only at the lunge's end) with a recovery turn (barely moving). Read the rhythm: in during recovery, out before the surge. Assess (§9) can reveal movement patterns, not just tells.

**The triangle:**
- **Trade** — stand and swing. Fast damage, eat hits.
- **Parry** — high risk, high reward. Viscerals and weak points; a whiff hurts.
- **Kite** — move and chip. Safe, slow, no openings, useless against anything as fast as you.

Good play flows between all three as health, charges, and tempo shift.

---

## 8. Action Economy & Items

Three pools per turn, plus one category outside them all.

   261	
   262	Three pools per turn, plus one category outside them all.
   263	
   264	**Movement** — your budget, splittable around your Action. Assess costs 1m from this pool.
   265	
   266	**Action** (one per turn):
   267	- Attack (light or heavy)
   268	- Hold (commit to parry on the enemy's turn)
   269	- Use an Action Item
   270	- Manage a track (smother, scrape, pressure)
   271	
   272	**Fast Action** (one per turn):
   273	- Use a Quick Item
   274	
   275	Heal-and-attack is real: Quick Items are Fast Actions, so vial-and-swing works. The lever is potency — Quick Items are deliberately weak; anything strong costs a full Action, competing with attacking. Big heal OR attack; small heal AND attack.
   276	
   277	**Reactions** sit outside all pools — things that happen on someone else's turn. Retreat (§12) and Abandon-All (below) live here.
   278	
   279	### Limb-gating
   280	
   281	| Requires | Staggered | Severed |
   282	|---|---|---|
   283	| Weapon arm (attacks, holds, item use) | −1 die / heavies disabled this turn | Can't wield that side; off-hand only, reduced damage, two-handers unusable |
   284	| Legs (movement) | Budget halved | Crawl only (1m), can't kite, prone (standing costs your Action) |
   285	| Head | Dazed — Discombobulation 1 turn, can't Assess | Dead (head severance always lethal) |
   286	| Torso | n/a — torso stagger is just heavy damage | Dead |
   287	
   288	A staggered leg takes kiting off the table — the triangle narrows by one option, yours or theirs.
   289	
   290	### Items
   291	
   292	**Quick Items** (Fast Action — weak, frequent):
   293	- **Wrap** — clears 2 Blood Loss stacks
   294	- **Salts** — clears 1 Insanity tick
   295	- **Tonic** — small flat heal (~10% max HP)
   296	- **Scour** — clears 1 Corrosion stack without costing your turn
   297	
   298	**Action Items** (full Action — strong, situational):
   299	- **Source-water flask** — big heal, but adds an Influence stack. Power for vulnerability.
   300	- **Cautery iron** — fully clears Blood Loss; stationary and exposed while using (no movement this turn).
   301	- **Thrown vial** — applies Corrosion/Burning at range.
   302	- **Charm** — one-shot buff (extra die, widened parry window); Insight-gated to use (§11).
   303	
   304	**Reactive / Instant** (usable anytime, even mid-enemy-turn, no slot):
   305	- **Abandon-All** — the panic word, the psychic tether home. Spoken or thought — needs only a working mind and voice, no limbs. Mechanically: Retreat (§12) — forfeit the Purse, keep character/limbs, instant, free, usable between any two actions including mid-resolution of an attack. The only counter — a gag/silence effect or an enemy that severed your voice — is rare, boss-tier, always telegraphed.
   306	- **Distress flare / ally beacon** — same category; multiplayer hook for later.
   307	
   308	**Passive / Equip** (no slot, always active) — trinkets and build-around gear. Modifies numbers only; often Insight-tier-gated to equip.
   309	
   310	---
   311	
   312	## 9. The Visceral System (parry + stopwatch)

A staggered leg takes kiting off the table — the triangle narrows by one option, yours or theirs.

input.old_str: **Budget** set by Endurance (5–11m). Spend before and after your action — in, strike, out — up to the total.
input.new_str:
**Budget** set by Endurance (8–14m). Movement is the single per-turn currency: repositioning *and* actions both draw from it. Spend before and after your action — in, strike, out — up to the total, minus whatever your action itself costs (§8).

**The hard rule:** if you can't pay an action's movement cost, you can't take it. Sprint the full budget and you've got no attack left in you this turn. Every metre run is a metre you can't fight with.

**Movement** — your budget, splittable around your Action. Actions cost movement (below); Fast Actions never do.

**Action** (one per turn — each costs movement from the budget, paid on use):

| Action | Movement cost | Fiction |
|---|---|---|
| Light attack | 1m | Plant feet, jab, keep moving |
| Heavy attack | 3m | Full stop, wind-up, commit |
| Hold (parry stance) | 2m | Set stance, weight down, wait |
| Manage a track (smother, scrape, pressure) | 1m | A pause, not a production |
| Use an Action Item | 2m | Hands busy, attention split |
| Assess | 1m | Reading, not acting |

Costs above are the defaults — specific weapons and actions may price differently, and perks may refund movement after an action, grant bonus movement before one, or zero a cost under conditions. (Perk design space, not yet populated.)

**Fast Action** (one per turn, always 0m):
- Use a Quick Item, load a capsule — anything done on the move. That's the whole point of the category.

530	2. **LUNGER** (elite) — HP ~500–700 · Move medium (5m). One telegraphed heavy + Blood Loss. Tell: raises blade to eye-line, steps in (~1.4s). Tier 1 — parry, free head. Teaches assess → hold → parry, and the reuse-a-tell trap if it has a second, faster jab.
   531	3. **BURSTER** (skirmisher) — HP ~400–600 · Move burst (8m surge / ~1m recover). Surges across the room, striking only at the lunge's end, then must recover. Tell: coils before the leap. Tier 1–2. The §7 dance enemy.
   532	4. **CHANTER** (caster) — HP ~300–500 · Move slow (3m), hangs back. No melee; ranged Influence + Insanity pressure each turn. Unparryable. Ignore it and the room gets worse — close and kill first. Low HP, soft limbs. The priority target.
   533	5. **SPITTER** (controller) — HP ~400–600 · Move medium (4m), keeps distance. Ranged Corrosion, stacks fast. Tell: swells, draws back (~1.6s — dodge or interrupt). Tier 1. The track-clock — close fast or get cornered and ticked to death. Pairs evilly with a Burster or Brute.
   534	6. **BRUTE** (heavy) — HP ~1500–2500 · Move slow (4m). Huge, slow heavies. Can amputate or bisect an under-levelled player on a fully-landed combo — telegraphed catastrophe, never random. Tell: overhead wind-up (~2.0s). Tier 2. Retreat is a valid answer. Sever a leg to neutralise its approach.
   535	7. **FLAILER** (chaotic) — HP ~600–900 · Move medium, erratic. Multiple whipping limbs, multi-track (Corrosion + Blood Loss). Tell: limbs draw back in unison — multiple windows in sequence (a ballistic chain). Tier 3. The precision-strike showcase; hardest non-boss to open.
   536	8. **EFFIGY** (duellist/mirror) — HP ~500–800 · Move fast (7m). Uses your toolkit: parries you, viscerals you, hit-and-runs, can out-move you. Fast, tight windows; reads your tells too. Tier 2–3. The fight that tests whether you've learned the system or just bullied mooks.
   537

   539	1. A gimmick punishing a default habit. (The Conjoined Twins punish centre-mass viscerals: low-HP trunk, and severing it splits them into two faster enemies.)
   540	2. A multi-parry ballistic chain (Tier 3) as the route to a real opening.

## 17. Running Notes — Turn Order, Recovery & Scaling

**Turn order.** Roll-off per encounter: d20, highest first, ties broken by Skill. No standing initiative.

**Out-of-combat recovery.** A quiet moment between fights (not the hub) allows a short rest: clear one track by a single stack, OR recover a small flat chunk of HP (~Quick Item tier). Free, no item spent. Does not restore visceral charges, limbs, or mutations — hub-exclusive.

**Scaling.** Enemy HP scales with player *level* (total points spent), not single-stat investment. Standard humanoid limb thresholds do NOT scale (§10). Bosses: HP and thresholds hand-set, exempt from the table. Rough HP multiplier on base table values — a rule of thumb, eyeball per encounter:

| Player level | Multiplier |
|---|---|
| 1–20 | ×1.0 |
| 21–40 | ×1.5 |
| 41–60 | ×2.25 |
| 61–80 | ×3.5 |
| 81–100 | ×5.0 |

**Insight-gain triggers.** Awarded at narratively significant moments, not on a checklist: first sighting of a new archetype, reading lore, surviving a near-death, tasting a dangerous source, defeating a boss. Usually +1 per trigger, once per discovery.

---

content.text:
605	**RALLY** — Damage taken → grey health, recoverable by landing a hit on your very next turn only. Default 50%; big telegraphed hits 75–100%, chip/DoT 0–25% — set per attack (§6).
   606	
   607	**TRACKS** (0–10 unless noted)
   608	
   609	| Track | Effect | Clears by |
   610	|---|---|---|
   611	| Blood Loss | Each new stack: +damage = new stack total. No passive drain. Cap 10 → Rupture | Bandage / cautery / pressure |
   612	| Rupture (triggered @ 10) | 25% Max HP damage + stagger + Blood Loss resets | Resolves once |
   613	| Insanity | Fail Save Roll → DM controls you 1 turn. ↑ with Insight | Time / calm / items |
   614	| Burning | Damage/turn, spreads to flammables | Smother (action) |
   615	| Discombobulation (Tick, 1–3) | Bad exits, reversed move, lost reactions | −1/turn, automatic |
   616	| Influence | Fail Save Roll → obey/compelled effect next turn, severity scales with stacks (DM discretion), uncapped past 10. ↑ Insanity & Insight | Full source elimination (ALL active sources if multiple) |
   617	| Corrosion | stacks × 2 dmg/turn, cap 5 | Manage action → −1/turn; else holds |
   618	| Fugue (on death) | Insight reads become false | Spend next Insight earned (not banked) |
   619	
   620	**SAVE ROLL** — d20 ≥ 10 + track value − ⌊Resolve ÷ 10⌋ + ⌊Insight ÷ 2⌋ (+ mods). One formula, both saves (§5).
   621	
   622	**MOVEMENT** — Budget 5–11m (Endurance), splittable around your action. Trade / Parry / Kite triangle. Out-speed slow enemies; dance burst-movers (in on recovery, out before surge). Hit-and-run = strike + retreat, no parry, no opening.
   623	
   624	**ACTION ECONOMY** — Action (attack / hold / Action Item / manage track) + Fast Action (Quick Item) + Movement. Quick Items weak but free alongside an attack; Action Items strong but cost the turn. Limb-gating table in §8. Abandon-All = Retreat, voice/mind only, exempt from the economy — stoppable only by rare, telegraphed boss-tier silence.
   625	
   626	**PARRY/VISCERAL** — Assess (1m) reads tell + timing (clarity scales with Insight). Hold → stopwatch in window, no charge cost, no cap on attempts. In = visceral (1.5× + knockdown + enemy loses move) ± mods. Out = eat heavy + punish. Don't reuse a tell. Some enemies retaliate.
   627	
   628	**POST-PARRY TIERS** — T1: free weak point, no watch. T2: widened precision watch. T3: parry watch + full precision watch.
   629	
   630	**LIMBS** — (multiplier / threshold). Effective dmg = raw × mult, fills meter + chips HP. Threshold over several hits = stagger (the triggering hit grants an immediate free called shot; enemy stagger duration is variable, set per Monster Template); in one hit = sever (permanent). Small high-value targets (bells, cracks, eyes) are just limbs with a high multiplier — no separate system. Torso or head severed = dead — but so is 0 HP from plain attacks, always. Specific-limb shots need an opening; default = torso. Self-initiated blind shots (no enemy tell) use one tolerance band tighter and end the turn on a miss.
   631	
   632	**PRECISION** (limbs >1.0 only) — tighter window for higher multiplier (head ±0.13s). Miss → progressively worse (chip / off-balance / wide + reactive / exposed). Removed by T1 parry, widened by T2.
   633	
   634	**MUTATION** — Lost limbs regrow at hub, chance of coming back changed (+power / −something). Read trades only at high Insight. Revert or reroll at hub, Vault White Salts.
   635	
   636	**RETREAT** — Between any actions → hub. Keep character/limbs, forfeit Purse. Free.
   637	
   638	**DEATH** — Respawn in the salt room you entered from. Keep character/levels/mutations. Gear forfeit to killer (a guaranteed spawn until killed; doesn't compound). Purse lost, Vault safe. Fugue applied.
   639	
   640	**SALTS ROOMS** — ~6 rooms. Recipe = biome (permanent constant); layout/loot/spawns reroll per entry. Salts single-use on activation; active room free to reuse until deliberately cleared (no refund). Clean exit via anchor (rerolls position, always exists) → Purse banks. Some recipes Insight-gated. Boss kills permanent; one landmark settles.
   641	
   642	**INSIGHT** — Earned, 0–10, five tiers. Sight + visceral mods + mutation-reading. ↑ power, ↑ Insanity/Influence floor. Spendable (doors, Insight-priced gear, ally summon at default 2) — spending drops score, tier, and floor together.
   643	
   644	---
   645	
[647 lines total]

display_content.json_block:
{"language": "markdown", "code": "**RALLY** — Damage taken → grey health, recoverable by landing a hit on your very next turn only. Default 50%; big telegraphed hits 75–100%, chip/DoT 0–25% — set per attack (§6).

**TRACKS** (0–10 unless noted)

| Track | Effect | Clears by |
|---|---|---|
| Blood Loss | Each new stack: +damage = new stack total. No passive drain. Cap 10 → Rupture | Bandage / cautery / pressure |
| Rupture (triggered @ 10) | 25% Max HP damage + stagger + Blood Loss resets | Resolves once |
| Insanity | Fail Save Roll → DM controls you 1 turn. ↑ with Insight | Time / calm / items |
| Burning | Damage/turn, spreads to flammables | Smother (action) |
| Discombobulation (Tick, 1–3) | Bad exits, reversed move, lost reactions | −1/turn, automatic |
| Influence | Fail Save Roll → obey/compelled effect next turn, severity scales with stacks (DM discretion), uncapped past 10. ↑ Insanity & Insight | Full source elimination (ALL active sources if multiple) |
| Corrosion | stacks × 2 dmg/turn, cap 5 | Manage action → −1/turn; else holds |
| Fugue (on death) | Insight reads become false | Spend next Insight earned (not banked) |

**SAVE ROLL** — d20 ≥ 10 + track value − ⌊Resolve ÷ 10⌋ + ⌊Insight ÷ 2⌋ (+ mods). One formula, both saves (§5).

**MOVEMENT** — Budget 5–11m (Endurance), splittable around your action. Trade / Parry / Kite triangle. Out-speed slow enemies; dance burst-movers (in on recovery, out before surge). Hit-and-run = strike + retreat, no parry, no opening.

**ACTION ECONOMY** — Action (attack / hold / Action Item / manage track) + Fast Action (Quick Item) + Movement. Quick Items weak but free alongside an attack; Action Items strong but cost the turn. Limb-gating table in §8. Abandon-All = Retreat, voice/mind only, exempt from the economy — stoppable only by rare, telegraphed boss-tier silence.

**PARRY/VISCERAL** — Assess (1m) reads tell + timing (clarity scales with Insight). Hold → stopwatch in window, no charge cost, no cap on attempts. In = visceral (1.5× + knockdown + enemy loses move) ± mods. Out = eat heavy + punish. Don't reuse a tell. Some enemies retaliate.

**POST-PARRY TIERS** — T1: free weak point, no watch. T2: widened precision watch. T3: parry watch + full precision watch.

**LIMBS** — (multiplier / threshold). Effective dmg = raw × mult, fills meter + chips HP. Threshold over several hits = stagger (the triggering hit grants an immediate free called shot; enemy stagger duration is variable, set per Monster Template); in one hit = sever (permanent). Small high-value targets (bells, cracks, eyes) are just limbs with a high multiplier — no separate system. Torso or head severed = dead — but so is 0 HP from plain attacks, always. Specific-limb shots need an opening; default = torso. Self-initiated blind shots (no enemy tell) use one tolerance band tighter and end the turn on a miss.

**PRECISION** (limbs >1.0 only) — tighter window for higher multiplier (head ±0.13s). Miss → progressively worse (chip / off-balance / wide + reactive / exposed). Removed by T1 parry, widened by T2.

**MUTATION** — Lost limbs regrow at hub, chance of coming back changed (+power / −something). Read trades only at high Insight. Revert or reroll at hub, Vault White Salts.

**RETREAT** — Between any actions → hub. Keep character/limbs, forfeit Purse. Free.

**DEATH** — Respawn in the salt room you entered from. Keep character/levels/mutations. Gear forfeit to killer (a guaranteed spawn until killed; doesn't compound). Purse lost, Vault safe. Fugue applied.

**SALTS ROOMS** — ~6 rooms. Recipe = biome (permanent constant); layout/loot/spawns reroll per entry. Salts single-use on activation; active room free to reuse until deliberately cleared (no refund). Clean exit via anchor (rerolls position, always exists) → Purse banks. Some recipes Insight-gated. Boss kills permanent; one landmark settles.

**INSIGHT** — Earned, 0–10, five tiers. Sight + visceral mods + mutation-reading. ↑ power, ↑ Insanity/Influence floor. Spendable (doors, Insight-priced gear, ally summon at default 2) — spending drops score, tier, and floor together.

---

summaries.summary: Reconciling mechanical inconsistencies between game sections.

content.text:
Pushed — both changes live on GitHub, dev log updated.

One knock-on I noticed but didn't touch: the monster table's move budgets were tuned against a 5m player (Burster's 8m surge used to out-pace you; now it just matches your base). Might want an enemy speed pass eventually — flagged in my head, not in the docs, since it's tuning rather than rules. Say the word if you want it logged properly.

**Back to the gallery.** Lloyd's at melee range, creature's got 584 HP and 1 Discombobulation still ticking. Under the new economy his turn looks like: 8m budget, Heavy costs 3m, Light costs 1m, capsule load is 0m Fast Action — so Heavy + capsule + 5m of retreat clears the reach line with a metre spare.

What's the move?
what's the distance to the smaller doorway?

summaries.summary: Triangulating spatial positioning and distance measurements.
Visualized spatial positioning and calculated doorway distance.

content.text:
Placing it concretely, since it matters now: the low doorway sits on the far side of the creature's position, about **6m from where Lloyd's standing** — and crucially, roughly 2–3m of that path runs *inside* the reach zone, because the doorway itself is within about 3m of whatever it's rooted to. That's why it read as "close, less muffled" when he first approached from the other side.

So under the new economy: reaching the doorway this turn is affordable (6m of his 8m budget), even with a Light jab on the way past (1m more). But it means being inside its reach when his turn ends — right as its Discombobulation ticks out — and ducking through a low doorway with an angry thing's tendrils in range behind him.

Alternative reads: retreat out of reach entirely and keep chipping from safety (it can't follow), or burn it down more before making the doorway run.

What's the call?
Lloyd isn't going to win the fight, so he takes his shot and runs for the doorway, using all his movement this turn to do so. he loads another cartridge into his knuckle as he does, just in case

summaries.summary: Thinking about resolving Lloyd's tactical retreat and combat actions.
Lloyd fleeing doorway while creature readies counterattack.
Calculated escape route amid critical health peril.

content.text:
The jab lands on his way past — **30 damage, 584 → 554** — and he sprints the arc, 7 of 8 metres spent, capsule clicking home mid-stride (Fast Action, 0m — that's exactly what the category's for). Capsules at **1/4 remaining, one loaded.**

He reaches the doorway. But his turn ends there — crouched at the threshold, still inside that last metre of its reach — and the Discombobulation ticks out with him standing in the worst spot to be standing.

**Creature's turn.** Clear-headed now, and he's in range. The grab lands — tendrils snap around his ankle right as he's ducking under the lintel. **35 damage: 73 → 38 HP.** And it *pulls* — he's **Dragged** again, being hauled back out of the doorway toward the water.

Here's where loading that capsule "just in case" turns out to be the smartest thing he's done all night: the established break is a Heavy on the grip — 3m under the new economy, fresh 8m budget next turn, entirely affordable — and there's a live capsule already sitting in the chamber for it.

Your turn. Heavy the tendril and detonate, or something else?
double check Lloyd's health for me - i think its wrong. Anyway, he does a heavy punch on the tentacle as it drags him again, and legs it through the doorway, out of reach of the creature, right?

summaries.summary: Thinking about verifying character health status and narrative action sequence.
Recalculating grey health recovery from grab damage mechanics.
Reconciled health calculations and verified combat mechanics.

content.text:
Good catch — it's wrong because I forgot **Rally** entirely. §6: damage taken goes to grey health, recoverable by landing a hit on your very next turn, default 50%.

Recomputing: first grab dealt 35 → 73, with 17 grey. Lloyd landed the Heavy the very next turn — that's a Rally recovery I never credited. He should've been at **90**, not 73. Nothing hit him during the jab-and-run turn, so this second grab takes him 90 − 35 = **55 HP**, with another 17 sitting grey right now — recoverable *this turn* if he lands the hit he's about to throw.

So: **55 HP, 17 grey pending.** My apologies — two HP errors in one fight is a pattern worth me watching.

**The turn:** Heavy on the tendril (3m), capsule's loaded — stopwatch check, same self-initiated window: **1.0s, ±0.15s.**

- **Hit:** 57 + 50% = 86 damage, +1 Discombobulation, grip breaks, Rally recovers 17 → **72 HP**
- **Miss:** base 57 still lands, grip still breaks, Rally still triggers → 72 HP, but 1 self-inflicted Blood Loss stack (+1 damage → 71)

Either way he's free with 5m left — more than enough to duck through and clear the reach zone. The doorway's his.

Run the stopwatch.
0.88s. in the window again

0.88 — inside again. He's three for three on the stopwatch tonight.

The detonation blows the grip apart at the point of contact — **86 damage, 554 → 468** — and the tendril spasms back into the water, another Discombobulation stack scrambling whatever passes for its bearings. Rally recovers his grey: **72 HP.** Capsules: **0 loaded, 1/4 in reserve.**

Lloyd doesn't wait to watch. Five metres through the low doorway, under the lintel, and out the other side — past the reach line, clear.

Behind him: thrashing, wet and furious, receding as the acoustics close in around him. It can't follow. It never could.

The passage beyond the doorway is tight for the first stretch, then opens out. The air changes — drier, colder, and carrying something new: a faint mineral smell, sharp, almost clean. Given what he's down here looking for, "clean" is an interesting word for anything to smell like.

The passage forks ahead: the cold mineral air is coming from the left. The right continues level and unremarkable.

Left toward the clean smell, or right?
Clean smell

The left passage descends in shallow, deliberate steps — cut stone, not worn rock, someone *built* this approach. The mineral smell sharpens with every step down until it's almost antiseptic, cutting through the wet-rot baseline this whole place breathes.

The stairs open into a small round chamber, and there it is.

A **basin of pale stone** in the room's center, waist-high, and rising from its middle: the **anchor** — this run's clean chalice. Still, silvery water fills the basin around its base, unnaturally clear, the source of the smell. The whole chamber is quiet in the way the chapel was quiet, but without the sense of being watched. This room reads *neutral*. Maybe the only genuinely neutral place in the biome.

Per the rules: touching the anchor is the clean exit — Purse banks, layout rerolls behind him. Everything he's carrying — 6 White Salts, the idol, the wooden token — goes home safe.

Nothing guards it. Nothing's hidden that his eyes can find. It's just here, the way the exit always is, somewhere.

Two hanging threads if he leaves now: the untaken right fork, and the corridor fork from earlier. Both survive the reroll as concepts, but this layout dies when he touches it.

Bank it and go home, or push his luck on the right fork first — 72 HP, one capsule left?
lets check out the right fork now that we know where our exit is

Lloyd backtracks up the cut steps and takes the right fork — level, unremarkable, exactly as advertised, right up until it isn't.

The passage ends at a **collapsed section**: ceiling's come down, mostly blocking the way, but there's a crawl-gap along the left side, big enough for a person, dark past the first metre. And someone's been through it before — the rubble at the gap's mouth is disturbed in a way that reads *repeated use*, not one desperate scramble. A faint path worn through the debris.

From the gap: warmth. Actual warmth, the first he's felt since the salts room steam. And very faintly, intermittently — a sound he takes a second to place, because it doesn't belong down here.

Someone humming. Off-key, unhurried. The way people hum when they think they're alone.

Crawl through, call out first, or head back to the anchor and bank while the run's clean?
he crawls through, trying to be silent

Under the new table that's a d20 **+0** (Skill 17) — squeezing through a rubble crawl without announcing yourself is exactly the uncertain-action clause. The disturbed debris cuts both ways: a worn path means less loose scree to betray him. **DC 11.**

Roll it.

11 exactly meets DC 11 — passes, barely. The kind of quiet that involves one held breath and a rock that rolled but didn't clatter.

Lloyd emerges into a pocket of survived architecture — a room the collapse spared. And it's *lived in*: a low fire in a scraped-out hearth, bedding made from what looks like chapel bench cushions, cookware that's been mended more than once. Years of habitation, not weeks.

The hummer has his back to the gap — an **old man**, thin, wrapped in layered robes gone the color of the stone, tending something over the fire. Still humming. Genuinely hasn't heard Lloyd come through.

And Lloyd's seen him before. Or — someone like him. The old man behind the Insight-gated door in the flooded colonnade, last layout. Same build, same robes. But that door needed Insight 6 to open, the colonnade got rerolled with everything else, and yet here he is, on the wrong side of a collapse, in a completely different layout, humming over a fire.

Whatever the reroll does to this place, it apparently doesn't do it to *him*.

Lloyd's unnoticed, crouched at the gap. Announce himself, watch a while, or back out quietly?
"Howdy, stranger," he says to the man, standing up and making himself known

The humming stops mid-phrase. The old man doesn't startle — doesn't even turn at first, just finishes stirring whatever's over the fire with the unhurried care of someone who decided a long time ago that being snuck up on wasn't worth the energy of fearing.

"Hm. Through the crawl, and quietly too." He turns. His face is deeply lined but his eyes are sharp — and they go, immediately and without apology, to Lloyd's pocket. The one with the idol in it. "You've been to the chapel."

Not a question. He gestures, loosely, at the fire and the general vicinity of a second seat-shaped rock that suggests he's had guests before, or at least optimism.

"Sit if you like. You're the first in a while to come through the polite way — most everything else uses the water." A pause, and then, conversational but not casual: "It didn't ask your name, did it? The thing in the niche. It does that, with some. Nod or shake, don't say — names carry down here."

He waits. There's no menace in him that Lloyd can read — but the journal's warning, the *"R. says it only wants to be asked in"* line, and a man who somehow persists across rerolls all belong to the same growing pile of things that don't add up yet.

How's Lloyd playing it?

"You mean the idol? It didn't speak to me, no."

Is Lloyd's Insight correct atm? Did he get +1 for that chained blob thing he just got past?

summaries.summary: Awarding Insight point for creature encounter scouting.

content.text:
Good catch — no, I hadn't awarded it, and it's owed. Ruling per §17's triggers:

- **+1: first sighting of a new archetype** — the rooted dragger was scouted, Assessed, fought, and escaped. Clear-cut.
- **+1: reading lore** — listed verbatim as a trigger, and the burned journal was substantive (three real revelations). This one's more borderline since the rules don't define how much lore counts, but I'll stand by it.

**Insight 4 → 6.** Which is suddenly a big deal: 6 is exactly the Charm's use-gate, and it was the threshold on that Insight-locked door in the old colonnade — the one this man was behind. The Charm is now live whenever Lloyd wants to burn it.

---

The old man's eyes crinkle at the answer. "Didn't *speak*." He says it back like he's weighing the words. "No. It wouldn't, not out loud, not the first time. It asks other ways — a want to tell it something, an itch to introduce yourself. If you didn't feel that, you're either lucky or it wasn't hungry."

He ladles something from the pot into a dented cup and, notably, does not offer any.

"You took it, though. It's in your pocket, and the pocket's warm — that's it liking being carried." He sips. "I'll trade you plain truth for plain truth, boy. One question honest for one question honest, as many rounds as you like. Only rule's the one I gave you — no names. Not mine, not yours, not down here."

First honest question's Lloyd's, if he takes the deal.
He nods. "What use does this idol have to me, if any?"

summaries.summary: Thinking about interpreting dialogue and responding to a character's question about an object's utility.
Weighing which insight best addresses the idol's utility.
Architected idol mechanics balancing player agency against escalating personal cost.

content.text:
The old man holds his hand out — not to take it, just to see it. Lloyd shows him without letting go, and the man nods like it confirms something.

"That's a Beckoner. Chapel-folk carved them to invite things in — that's the pose, palms up, *come in, be welcome*. Point it at a thing and mean it, and it does the asking on your behalf." He taps his own temple. "The pull the water-things and the singers put on *you*? It puts that on *them*. Useful, if you can stomach the price."

He sips, unhurried. "The price is it keeps the door open both ways. Every time you use it, it wants a little more to know who's carrying it. That's why the note-writers all died mid-sentence, boy — it asks, eventually, and the ones who answer aren't carried out."

**Mechanically — Idol identified (via lore, not Insight roll): "The Beckoner," Action Item (2m under the new economy):**
- Point it at one creature in line of sight and speak an invitation: target takes **1 Influence stack** — your Influence rules, mirrored back at them. Stacks build per use, saves and compulsions per the standard track, severity scaling as normal.
- **Cost:** each use puts **1 Influence stack on Lloyd**, with the idol itself as the source — and the source-elimination clear rule means those stacks don't clear while he's carrying it. Drop it or Vault it, they clear. Keep using it, they build.

His honest answer's given. The man refills his cup. "My turn. What did you do to the dragger in the flood gallery? It's been *loud* the last hour, and it's never loud."
Lloyd laughs at such a simple question. "Oh, I, um... Punched it, really hard, a couple of times."

The old man barks a laugh — a genuine, rusty thing that turns into a brief cough. "*Punched* it. Gods." He shakes his head, wiping his eye. "Forty years of things sneaking past it, tricking it, bribing it with corpses — and along comes a boy who just hits it. It'll sulk about that for a month. It's proud, in its way."

He settles, still visibly delighted. "That was cheap of me as questions go. Ask yours."

The rhythm of the trade is established now, and the pile of unanswered things is deep: how he persists when the layouts reroll, who "R." was, what the dragger's actually looking for, what the clerk upstairs meant about *your brother*, what the Choir Deep recipe leads to, why names carry down here. Or something else entirely.

What's Lloyd's next question?
Lloyd eyes the man, suspicious. He already asked his question.

The old man catches the look and raises both palms, cup and all, unhurried.

"The dragger question, aye. That was mine, and you answered it. Fair's fair." He gestures at Lloyd with the cup, granting the floor. "Yours now. I don't cheat the trade, boy — the trade's the only thing down here that's kept me honest, and honest is most of why I'm still breathing."

The fire pops. He waits, watching Lloyd over the rim of the cup — not impatient, just an old man with all the time this place has, which appears to be all of it.

So — the question is Lloyd's, whenever he picks one. Persistence across rerolls, "R.", the dragger's search, *your brother*, Choir Deep, why names carry, or a curveball of his own.
no, Lloyd already asked his question, about the idol.

content.thinking: I'm recounting the exchange to make sure I have the turn order right — Lloyd asked about the idol and got an answer, then the old man asked about the dragger and Lloyd answered, so that completes one full round. Now it should be Lloyd's turn again for round two, but the user is pushing back on my understanding of whose turn it actually is. I see the issue now — Lloyd already posed his question about the idol, so it's the old man's turn to ask something next, not Lloyd's turn again. The exchange should alternate: Lloyd asks, the old man answers, then the old man asks, Lloyd answers. I jumped ahead by prompting Lloyd for another question when I should've had the old man pose his next question instead. Let me have the old man ask Lloyd something now.

summaries.summary: Verifying turn order and confirming Lloyd's next question.

content.text:
Right, my mistake — the round was Lloyd asks, man answers, man asks, Lloyd answers. That's one complete cycle. It's the old man's turn to open the next one, not Lloyd's.

He sets his cup down properly this time, like he's actually choosing the question rather than lobbing something easy.

"Alright. Real one, then." He studies Lloyd a moment. "That clerk upstairs — the one who does the Vault. She say anything to you about a brother?"

He's watching for the reaction more than waiting for words.

(oh my god i just reread your narration - as many rounds as we like. I thought he said only one. I'm an idiot)

Lloyd nods, yes. "She's mentioned a brother twice now." Lloyd looks at the Idol, wary of saying too much. He doesn't say aloud whether he does or doesn't have a brother. 

"My turn. Is there any way to kill the dragger-thing back there that isn't going to cost me an arm and a leg?"

content.thinking:
The old man needs to give Lloyd a straight answer about taking down the dragger—honest tactical advice that fits their "plain truth for plain truth" agreement. Since the creature is rooted in place, already damaged down to around 584 HP from its original 700, and has no exploitable weaknesses, the practical answer is that it's a grinding fight: sustained ranged attacks or hit-and-run tactics are the only viable approach, with no shortcuts available. The old man should give an honest answer grounded in what he actually knows: he doesn't have a clean trick, but he can explain what the creature is doing (still searching for something it lost, dragging corpses in hope) and suggest that HP attrition is the real path forward, or hint that fire might work well against something waterlogged and rooted — flavorful without being a cheat code, and he can safely kite it since it's anchored in place.

Actually, there's a better mechanical answer: thrown vials like Corrosion or Burning are Action Items that can be used from range without needing to get close, letting Lloyd apply chip damage or stacking status effects from complete safety. The old man wouldn't know the exact game terms, but he could offer folk wisdom about "things that burn or eat away that don't need you close" — which translates naturally into using vials as a safe, resource-based solution. So I'll frame this as the old man speaking from experience—grounded, practical advice that acknowledges the player's situation without turning it into a mechanics lecture.

summaries.summary: Thinking about continuing a collaborative fantasy roleplay narrative.
Weighing practical tactics against the dragger's rooted vulnerability.
Weighed practical combat tactics and resource-based solutions for attrition.

content.text:
He considers the question longer than the last one — actually thinking, not performing it.

"Cheap, no. Cheaper than what you've been doing, maybe." He pokes the fire. "It's rooted. Can't come to you, can't chase you, can't do a thing about anything that hurts it from outside its own reach. You've already learned exactly how far that reach goes, yeah?"

He glances at Lloyd's pockets — not the idol this time, more general. "Anything you've got that burns or eats through things doesn't care about distance the way your fists do. Throw that instead of carrying it in on your knuckles, and it just... sits there, taking it, forever, unable to do a single thing back. No risk on your end at all, past the throwing arm."

He shrugs. "Slow. Ugly. Not glorious. But it's rooted and you're not, and that's the whole fight in one sentence, if you ask me. HP is HP — there's no secret door in the thing, no weak point I know of. Just whether you're standing somewhere it can touch you while you take it down."

He looks back to the fire. "Your question's spent. Mine, if you'll still trade — what's the Choir Deep for? You've had that recipe a while and haven't brewed it."

Lloyd shrugs. Another strange, simple question. "Brew it into one of the Salt Rooms in Stillwell Hydro and it'll take you somewhere else. Wherever it takes you, you fight through, drink from the clean chalice, and return to the Hydro with whatever you found while you were wherever it sent you. I don't know where it'll take me yet."

content.thinking: Lloyd's had his turn to answer, so now I should give him the chance to ask his question and keep the exchange moving forward naturally.

summaries.summary: Thinking about interpreting a fictional magical system and its mechanics.
Yielded narrative turn to advance conversational momentum.

content.text:
The old man nods slowly at that, like it confirms something rather than teaches him anything new. "Recipes and clean chalices. Same as ever, then — just new shapes for it." He doesn't ask which recipe it is, doesn't seem to need to.

He gestures at Lloyd, floor open again. "Round's yours."

The pile's still sitting there — how he persists across the rerolls, who "R." was, what the dragger's actually searching for all these years, why names carry weight down here, what's really behind the Choir Deep once he brews it, or something new entirely. Or he could let the trade rest and just talk plainly about getting out, given the anchor's already found and the Purse is sitting there waiting to be banked.

What's Lloyd asking, or is he done trading for now?
"New shapes - what were they when you were ... doing this?"

content.thinking:
The question is probing at the old man's past, suggesting he was once an adventurer himself before settling here. Given that he persists across rerolls and seems knowledgeable about the game systems, he might be a former player-character who got trapped in this world—an in-world explanation for why this NPC behaves differently from the procedurally generated ones.

This is a chance for an evocative lore reveal that deepens the mystery without over-explaining. Following the soulslike design philosophy of fragmented, ambiguous storytelling, I should give a partial answer that raises more questions than it answers. The old man's expression shifts to something wistful as he describes how the ritual used to have different names and forms, but the core structure remained the same: descend, survive, drink, return diminished. He might hint that he was once a Salter too, but something went wrong—maybe he never truly came back, or drank from the wrong chalice, leaving him trapped in this liminal space across rerolls.

summaries.summary: Thinking about clarifying an incomplete or fragmented question about shapes.
Theorizing old man's past as trapped player-character.
Wove fragmented lore revealing the old man's mysterious past involvement.

content.text:
Something in his face shifts — not surprised, just tired in a way it wasn't a second ago.

"Different words. Different water." He turns his cup slowly, not drinking. "No salt rooms, no chalices, not by those names. But you brew something, or you're given something, and it takes you somewhere that isn't where you started, and you fight through it, and there's always a *clean thing* at the end you have to touch to come home properly. That part doesn't change. Only the dressing does."

He looks at the fire instead of Lloyd now. "I did it more times than I can rightly count. Came back every time. Vault got fatter, room got fuller." A gesture, vague, at the cushions and the mended cookware. "Until the time I didn't come back *properly*. Touched the clean thing same as always. Just didn't end up back at any Hydro."

He doesn't elaborate on what "properly" failed to mean. His eyes come back to Lloyd, and there's something almost like a warning in them, gentle as it is.

"Rerolls don't touch me anymore. Whatever I am now, I'm not one of the things that gets reshuffled with the room." A thin, humorless smile. "Small mercies. I get to keep my fire."

He picks his cup back up, done with that thread whether Lloyd pushes it or not. "Your question was free, that one — I didn't take one back. Trade's still open if you want it, or you've got a Purse waiting and an anchor that isn't going anywhere either."
Lloyd's eyes narrow. "Does that mean you have a stash back out in the real world that you don't need? I'll take it off your hands, if you'd like."

content.thinking:
The old man is being directly asked for something without going through the honest-question trade, which is more of a social negotiation than a mechanical moment. Given his characterization—mysterious, amused by Lloyd, and bound by his own rules about honesty—he'd likely respond with some mixture of amusement and refusal, staying true to his nature while not being outright hostile.

I don't think this needs a roll; it's just him deciding how to respond in character. He probably has nothing left to give since he's stuck here permanently and whatever he once had went into the room itself (the cushions, the mended cookware) from his old Salt runs. The best response feels like dry humor at Lloyd's audacity, maybe a deflection about his situation, and either a small token as a joke or a firm "no" with an explanation. I'll keep it brief and punchy like Jake's style, maintain the dry tone, and leave room for Lloyd's next move.

summaries.summary: Thinking about continuing a fictional dialogue scene.
Weighing the old man's amusement against his rule-bound nature.
Weighed character consistency against mechanical rules and opted for dry humor.

content.text:
That gets an actual laugh, bigger than the punching one. "Cheeky bastard." He wipes his eye again, delighted rather than offended. "No, boy — 'real world' isn't a shelf I've got access to anymore. Whatever I had before this fire, it's been fire-fuel, cushion stuffing, and pot-mending for longer than you've been alive. There's no 'out there' stash. This—" he gestures at the meager camp "—is the whole estate."

He considers Lloyd a moment longer, something calculating under the amusement now. "But I'll tell you what. You want something for your trouble, take *that* instead—" he nods at a small clay jar tucked near the hearth stones, unlabeled "—it's Source-water, proper stuff, not the fouled kind you'll find in the rooms. One good heal, no Influence tax attached to this batch. I've no more use for healing, given my circumstances."

He holds it out, actually offering this time. "Call it payment for making the dragger's week worse. I haven't laughed like that in longer than I'd like to admit."

**If taken: Source-water flask (clean variant) added — big heal, no Influence stack, unlike the standard version.**

Take it, push for more, or call it here and head for the anchor?
Lloyd accepts it, nods politely, thanks the man. "I'll keep seeing you if I use the same salt recipe?"

content.thinking:
I'm working through whether the old man is anchored to the first-biome recipe itself or exists independently of any specific recipe structure. He's already established that he's not subject to layout rerolls, but the question is whether he's permanently bound to that initial salt room recipe or if he could exist outside of it entirely.

Looking at the evidence: both encounters happened within the same recipe despite different layouts, which supports the idea that he's tied to the recipe rather than the room's geometry. But whether he'd persist in a completely different recipe like Choir Deep remains genuinely unknown—he's never left this one to find out. I should have him acknowledge this uncertainty honestly rather than claiming knowledge he doesn't have, keeping the mystery alive while giving a grounded answer about what he does know.

summaries.summary: Thinking about interpreting dialogue context and Lloyd's question about salt recipe consistency.
Determining whether the old man binds permanently to recipes.
Weighed recipe-binding versus independence, acknowledged genuine uncertainty.

content.text:
He tilts his head, actually considering it rather than answering by rote. "Same recipe's the only thing I've tested, boy — twice now, with you, and however many times before you without you knowing it. Every reroll of *this* one, I'm still here, still by this fire, wherever the fire happens to land." He shrugs slightly. "Whether I'd turn up in a different brew entirely — new recipe, new dressing — I couldn't tell you. Never had reason to leave this one and find out. Might be I'm bound to it specifically. Might be I'm bound to something under all of them and this is just where I happen to sit."

He sips again, unbothered by his own uncertainty. "You'll find out before I will, most like, once you brew that Choir Deep of yours. Come tell me if you see my face somewhere else. I'd be curious too."

He settles back toward the fire, humming already starting to creep back in — conversation winding down on his end, if not necessarily Lloyd's.

Anything else before Lloyd heads for the anchor, or is he calling it here?
"Not a part of our game, but before I go - if I return, do you want me to bring you anything?"

content.thinking:
I'm trying to parse whether Jake is asking a meta question directly to me as the DM, or if Lloyd is speaking in-character to the old man but flagging it as outside the game's narrative scope. The phrasing "not a part of our game" suggests Jake might be breaking character to ask something about the game itself before wrapping up.

Actually, I think I've got it—Jake is clarifying that this is purely narrative flavor with no mechanical weight. He wants Lloyd to ask the old man if there's anything he'd like brought back next time, just as a roleplay gesture, not a rules test. So I should respond in-character as the old man answering that question, keeping it consistent with his personality and situation.

summaries.summary: Thinking about interpreting dialogue context and responding to an offer.
Deciphering whether Jake breaks character to ask meta-question.
Deciphered narrative intent and prepared in-character response.

content.text:
The old man looks almost caught off guard by that — not suspicious, just genuinely unused to being asked.

He thinks about it properly, the humming pausing again. "Kind of you to ask, actually." A small, real smile. "Nothing from the world topside — long past wanting for that. But if you happen across a source that runs clean, actually clean, not what usually passes for it down here—" he taps the little clay jar's cousin sitting empty by the pot "—bring me a taste of that, if it's no trouble. Been drinking recycled fear for longer than I can stomach thinking about. A clean sip of something wouldn't go astray."

He waves it off almost immediately after, like he's embarrassed to have wanted anything at all. "Don't go out of your way for it, mind. I've lasted this long without."

Back to humming, low, already half-elsewhere. Whenever Lloyd's ready, the anchor's waiting.
Lloyd heads back to the clean chalice, drinks and blacks out

content.thinking: Lloyd's returning to the anchor to drink from the chalice and wrap up the session—this feels like a natural narrative endpoint since he's found what he came for and explored thoroughly. The Purse banks everything he's collected: the six White Salts and the idol. I should check the anchor mechanics to confirm the reroll happens and see if Jake wants to call it here or push forward.

summaries.summary: Thinking about a narrative scene involving character intoxication and loss of consciousness.
Orchestrating Lloyd's climactic return and cataloging collected treasures.
Weighed narrative closure and mechanical confirmation before session's end.

content.text:
The chalice's water is cold going down — colder than water should be — and the round chamber loses its edges almost immediately after. No pain, no wrench, just a clean cut to black.

He comes to at Stillwell Hydro, upright, like he never fell. Whatever the crossing actually is, it's mercifully instant from the inside.

**Session tally:**
- **Purse banked:** 6 White Salts → Vault
- **Idol identified in-run:** *The Beckoner* — Action Item, 2m, forces an Influence stack onto a target; costs Lloyd 1 stack per use while carried, uncleared until dropped/Vaulted
- **Wooden Token** — keepsake, no mechanical effect
- **Source-water flask (clean variant)** — big heal, no Influence tax
- **Insight 4 → 6** (new archetype sighted + lore read) — **Charm is now usable**
- **Powder Charge capsules:** 1/4 remaining
- **HP:** 72/108, no active status tracks
- Choir Deep recipe still sitting unbrewed in his room

Loose threads still open: the old man's request for clean water, "R.," what the dragger's actually looking for, the Choir Deep unbrewed, and the Charm being live for the first time.

Is this where we're calling session 2, or is there more you want to get through tonight?
lets bank and update our sheets - returning to Hydro restores HP, right. does it also restore powder charge capsules?

content.thinking: I need to clarify whether full restoration at the hub covers consumable items like Powder Charge capsules or just restores HP, limbs, and mutations—I should double-check the exact wording in the Hub section and see what the weapon description says about capsule refills.

summaries.summary: Thinking about game mechanics for HP and powder charge restoration at Hydro location.

490	## 14. The Hub
   491	
   492	The only stable thing — and even it should be slightly wrong.
   493	
   494	- **Full restoration, always.** Whatever was torn off, home fixes it (regrowth per §10 — possibly changed).
   495	- **Banking.** Purse moves fully into Vault on arrival. Spend Vault White Salts on attribute levels (§4), mutation revert/reroll (§10), and other hub services.
   496	- Same room every visit, **one detail changed each time** — a clock never showing the same time twice, an NPC recalling a conversation you haven't had yet. Safe and faintly unnerving.
   497	- **The recurring antagonist:** one NPC reappearing across warped locations — always slightly different, always unhelpful or hostile, never explained. (In the starter location: the reception clerk.)

   504
